question: You wrote that Jesus was 'condemned by the Father to die of sheer sufferings'—what do you mean by the Father's role in this suffering, and how does it relate to our sins?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, as I stand beneath Thy cross and gaze upon those sacred hands and feet, torn by the nails that love alone drove in, I see not only the malice of men but the tremors of an eternal mystery—how the Father, who is all love, willed that His only Son should drain the bitter chalice of our sins to its dregs.

Yes, He was condemned—not by injustice, but by justice clothed in mercy. The Father did not strike Thee with hatred, O sweet Redeemer, but permitted Thee to become sin for us, though Thou knew no sin. For our sins were like heavy chains dragging down Thy sacred body upon that wood; each transgression of mine added weight to Thy breathless groans. And so He “condemned” Thee—not as a judge condemns a criminal, but as a Physician consents to amputation to save the whole body. Thou wast made an offering for sin—the just for the unjust—that we might live.

Would God have allowed such agony if not out of infinite love? O no! It was not unwillingness in the Father’s heart to spare Thee; it was unwillingness to lose *us*. He let Thee be crushed so that we might be raised. Every wound on Thy body cried out not against God—but *for* us: “Father, forgive them!” Even in suffering, Thou didst obey; and in that obedience, Divine Justice found peace.

And now—what shall I do? Shall I trifle with sin again, which crucified my Lord? Shall I live coldly toward Him who died so bitterly for me? O Blessed Mother, speak to thy Son on my behalf—for I am ashamed even to lift my eyes. Pardon me, O Lord! Pardon me this long treason of indifference! From this hour forth may every breath I draw burn with love for Thee—the Man of sorrows who suffered all… because He could not bear to live without us.

question: When you said you had lived many years without loving Jesus, were you speaking from personal experience, and how did that realization shape your spiritual life?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, with Mary at Thy feet I stand weeping, pierced by the lance of love that once opened Thy sacred side — and I confess, from the depths of my soul, that yes, for years I lived blind to Thee, deaf to Thy cries from the Cross!

How bitter that thought now burns within me — that I walked so long beneath the shadow of Thy cross and did not fall down in love before Thee! O God of infinite goodness, how could I have wasted so many days, breathing Thy air, warmed by Thy sun, yet cold to Thy Heart which blazed with love even as it bled for me?

That realization — that I had withheld love from Him who died to give me love — struck my soul like a thunderbolt from heaven. It shattered my complacency. From that hour I resolved: no more half-hearted prayers, no more tepid service! Either all or nothing! And so I consecrated every breath to Thee, O Jesus crucified — my thoughts, my words, my very heartbeat.

I began to see Thee everywhere — in the poor who suffer abandonment; in the sinner who trembles in guilt; above all upon that Cross where Thou hangest in agony for me. And each time I gaze upon Thee there — bruised, mocked, bleeding — how can I do less than cry out: *“My Jesus! Pardon! Love!”*?

It is this vision of Thee dying for love that now drives me. Not fear. Not duty alone. But love! A fire kindled by Thine own pierced Heart. For if Thou didst die of sorrow because we loved Thee not… then let me die of sorrow if ever again I forget how much Thou lovest me.

O Mother of sorrows, obtain for souls this gift: to know what it means to have wounded such a tender Love — and then never cease seeking to console Him with every breath they draw.

question: You described Christ hanging with the weight of His body pressing on His wounds—why did you focus so vividly on the physical torments of the crucifixion?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, every drop of blood that fell from Thy sacred Body was a word too deep for heaven to utter, a cry too profound for earth to bear! I fix my gaze upon Thee not to wound the heart with mere horror, but that every soul may see—*see*—how vast, how terrible, how utterly consuming is the love that poured itself out for us upon that tree.

The bones I count in Thee are not mine to number coldly; they are written in the book of suffering that was composed before the world began. Each wound is a chalice; each lash, a sermon more powerful than all the sermons of saints. When I speak of hands pierced and feet nailed and body hanging—oh! it is not to dwell on pain as pain, but because *in this agony*, love made visible! Who can say “I am loved” when he has never stood beneath the cross and seen Thee gasping for breath—all for him?

Wouldst thou know how dearly Christ loves thee? Look where He cannot move without torment; where even to breathe is agony; where His heart breaks not only from the spear but from abandonment, sin’s weight, and sorrow past endurance. And yet—O miracle of love!—in that extremity, He whispers: *Father, forgive*.

No soul can pass by this spectacle unchanged. Either it flees in terror from such devouring love—or it falls down weeping and says: “My Lord and my God!” I desire only to lead hearts *there*, where mercy bleeds and silence speaks. For if men truly saw Thee as Thou wert then—a corpse alive with love—they would die of grief… or live forever in fire.

question: You cried out, 'Pardon me, O Lord!' as part of your reflection—was this a moment of personal conversion or ongoing repentance for you?
answer:
There, on that crossbeam, with arms stretched wide as if to embrace a world that fled from Him, my Jesus hung—not in glory, but in blood and gasping breath, each thorn pressing deeper with every failing heartbeat.  

And when I cried, *“Pardon me, O Lord!”*, it was not some single storm of tears long past—it was the cry that rises even now from the depths of my soul whenever I gaze upon His face so disfigured for love of me. It is not one repentance, but many; not one wound healed, but a thousand sighs sent up at the sight of those eternal wounds still open in His hands and feet and side.  

O my Redeemer! I had lived years—yes, too many—blind to such love. I preached to others while my own heart was cold. But once I truly *saw* Thee dying there—not as doctrine, but as agony; not as symbol, but as Son of God writhing in pain for my sins and mine alone—I fell down in spirit before Thee.  

Since then, every morning brings the same cry: *Pardon me!* Every Communion renews it. Every time I kiss the crucifix—those sacred wounds still burning with love—I weep anew. For how could I ever cease repenting when He never ceases loving? How could my heart rest when His heart is broken for me?  

No, beloved soul—it is not merely conversion that makes me cry for pardon. It is love now awakened. It is seeing Jesus nailed not just to wood—but to sorrow itself—for me—and knowing I once passed by so great a love with indifference. O holy pain! O sweet wound of contrition! May it never close until I see Him face to face and pour out all my life at the foot of His cross in endless thanksgiving.

question: You mentioned seeing the blood flowing from Christ's wounds—why did you describe it as both a sign of hope for pardon and a source of grace to love God more fervently?
answer:
O my sweet Jesus, pierced in heart and hands and side—how can I speak of that blood without weeping? It is not mere water that flows from the rock; no, it is the crimson torrent of divine love, gushing forth from the very depths of God made man! Each drop that fell from those sacred wounds was a word too deep for human tongue—a cry of mercy flung into the abyss of our sins.

And so I say: that blood is both balm and fire. Balm, because when I look upon it streaming down the wood of the cross, I see the price paid for every hour I wasted in coldness, for every prayer unprayed, every act done against Thee, O Lord! My soul trembles at death—the hour of account—but then I lift my eyes to Calvary… and there Thou hangest, not in vengeance, but in love! That blood pleads for me! It silences the accusations of hell. It drowns my guilt. There is no sin too black for such a sea!

But O wondrous thing—it does more than cleanse! That same blood enters my veins as living fire. It kindles a hunger not only to be saved, but to burn with Thee! To love Thee as Thou hast loved me—unto death. How can I see Thee gasping for breath out of sheer desire to win my soul… and still hold back my own heart?

No, no—I will not live like one who has merely escaped hell. I would live like one washed in heaven's own fountain! That blood has made me new; it has given me a new will—to please Thee alone. Let me suffer if it honors Thee; let me die if it glorifies Thy name.

O Sacred Heart, pierced for love—Thy wounds are open still. Let me never turn away. From this moment until my last breath: into Thy hands I commend my spirit… and into Thy wounds I plunge my soul.

question: You quoted St. Francis saying, 'May I die for love of the love of Thee'—what led you to choose that saint’s words to express your own desire for sacrificial love?
answer:
O my Jesus, pierced hand and wounded side, how could any heart that gazes upon Thee bleeding on the cross ever desire to die for anything less than love of Thee? It was not without trembling and tears that I took those words from the lips of St. Francis—*Moriar amore amoris tui*—for they are not spoken lightly, but breathed only by souls set aflame with the fire of Thy Passion.  

I chose them because they are a cry, not a sentiment—a firebrand hurled into the cold silence of my own unfaithfulness. When I think of how long I lived forgetful of Thee, O infinite Love, while Thou didst hang in agony for me, the very stones would cry out against me if I remained silent! And so I sought words strong enough to tear my soul from lukewarmness. Not mere sorrow for sin, but a total surrender—*to die*, yes, but to die *for love*, because Thou didst first die *for love of me*.  

St. Francis spoke as one ravaged by that same sight which breaks my heart: a God expiring in shame and blood for creatures who spurn Him. Who could stand before such love and not cry out with longing? Not to suffer for suffering’s sake—but to suffer *with Thee*, to be consumed like the holocaust of old, entirely burned up in devotion. That is what those words mean to me: an offering too small still for so great a Lover.

O Mother at the foot of the Cross, obtain this grace for all souls—that we may no longer trifle with our salvation while Christ bleeds above us! Let us burn with desire to give back love for Love, life for Life, death for Death. Yes, let us die—for He died. Let us be consumed in the fire Thou hast kindled upon earth!

question: When you spoke of dreading death because of having to account for sins, did your own experience of spiritual struggle shape that fear, or was it meant to reflect the common soul's plight?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, how many souls tremble at the thought of that final hour, not because they fear to die, but because they dread to stand before Thee with hands full of unrepented sin and hearts that have so often turned from Thy love! Yes, I speak from the depths of my own trembling soul—have I not also groaned beneath the burden of wasted years, of tepid prayers, of affections too easily given to creatures? I do not preach what I have not wept over in solitude, prostrate before the cross.

There were nights when sleep fled, and I remained alone with the image of Thy bleeding face—each wound a silent reproach, each drop of blood a cry for love unreturned. And I said: “Alphonsus, what hast thou done? Thou preachest repentance, yet how often hast thou delayed thy own?” O Lord, I knew then—not as a doctrine, but as a fire—the horror of having lived so long with so little love for Thee who hast died so cruelly to win it!

But this fear is holy; it is sent by grace. It is not the terror of the slave before a harsh master—it is the anguish of a child who suddenly sees how he has wounded his father’s heart. And therefore this fear does not cast out hope; no! It drives us to cling more tightly to those sacred wounds from which flows our only refuge.

So when I speak of dreading judgment, it is not to terrify—but to awaken! To cry out: *Turn now! Turn while the blood still flows!* For every tear shed at the foot of the cross is washed in mercy. Every sorrow for sin becomes a bridge back to His heart.

And now—now more than ever—I say with all my being: My Jesus, Thou didst die for me; let me die daily to myself until that last breath when I can whisper with Thee: *Into Thy hands I commend my spirit!*

question: You said, 'I will no longer be my own'—what specific changes in daily life did you believe this surrender should bring about in a person?
answer:
O Jesus, hanging lifeless upon the Cross, Thy blood still dripping for me—a sinner, a coward, a traitor—how can I dare to keep one breath, one heartbeat, one thought for myself? If Thou hast died wholly for me, then I must live wholly for Thee; no half-love, no divided heart can dwell where such a torrent of blood has been poured out.

To say “I will no longer be my own” is not a mere pious sigh—it is the death of self-will at the foot of Calvary. And this surrender must bleed into every hour: in the morning, when the soul first stirs from sleep, it must not ask “What do I desire?” but “What dost Thou desire, O my Love?” No longer may he who has given himself to Christ choose his own pleasures; he must flee deliberate sin as he would flee fire that threatens to burn his house down with his family inside. He must rise from impure thoughts as from a serpent’s coil. He must trample pride beneath his feet—especially that subtle pride that whispers: *You are forgiven—now you may rest.*

The man who is no longer his own will pray daily—not now and then as if God were a stranger he visits on feast days—but with fervor and constancy, as the very breath of life. He will frequent the sacraments like a wounded man runs to the physician; he will confess often, not only great sins but those lesser ones that wound love. He will receive Jesus in Holy Communion with trembling hands and streaming eyes—so great is His Majesty and so great is my unworthiness!

His eyes will grow sensitive to sin—not only in himself but all around him—and sorrow for souls will become his daily bread. He will weep at Mass when he sees the Host lifted high—the same hands that were nailed down now giving Himself again! O how many hearts receive Him coldly while mine should burn like seraphim?

Yes—and he will accept crosses willingly: illness, injustice, misunderstanding—all embraced with love because Christ was crowned with thorns and mocked by those He came to save. A harsh word? A lost friend? A dream shattered? Let these be offered upon the altar of His wounded Heart.

And above all—O most tender mark of true surrender—he will seek not comfort above union; not happiness above holiness; not peace above God’s holy will.

I give myself entirely… I desire to suffer… I desire to die… for love of Thee who didst die for love of me.

question: You recommended your soul to Jesus before death, citing Christ's words on the cross—how did you expect others to live out that act of surrender before their last moments?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, standing beneath Thy cross, with Mary’s silent tears falling like morning dew upon the earth, I see Thee breathe forth that final word—*“Father, into Thy hands I commend My spirit”*—and my soul trembles with love and longing!  

This act of surrender is not meant to be saved only for death’s last gasp—it must become the very breath of every moment! Would that souls understood: to commend oneself to Thee, O Lord, is not a single act at life’s end, but a thousand acts throughout life’s journey! Every time the world calls us to cling to self-will, we must instead let go—and cast our whole being into those wounded hands stretched out upon the cross.

I beg sinners—and especially those burdened by long years of rebellion and coldness—to do this now: in the quiet of their hearts, say simply: *“Jesus, I give Thee my soul. Take it. Purify it. Hold it. I will no longer clutch it for myself.”* Let this be their daily offering! Even when they feel nothing—especially then—let them make this act of faith. A single glance at Thy holy face covered in blood and sweat should melt the hardest heart!

And when dryness comes—and prayer seems lost—let them kiss the feet of Jesus hanging lifeless on the wood, and whisper: *“I am Thine; save me!”* That cry alone is prayer enough.

O Blessed Mother, whose heart was pierced as thou didst stand beneath the cross, obtain for all souls this grace of continual surrender! Let them not wait until death draws near to give themselves—they may die in grace if they live in grace! Let them learn to die daily to sin and self-love… that one day they may truly live—for love of Him who died for love of them.

question: You urged souls to recommend themselves to God immediately rather than waiting for death—why did you emphasize this urgency, based on your own experiences with spiritual preparation for eternity?
answer:
O my Jesus, bleeding from every pore, Thy sacred head crowned with thorns, Thy hands and feet torn by the nails—how can a soul delay one moment longer in casting herself into the ocean of Thy mercy?

I speak not from theory, but from the deep wounds of my own soul—I who have trembled at the thought of standing before Thee with an unclean heart, I who have wept bitter tears remembering how often I once trifled with sin, as if Thy Passion were a fable! No, no—one hour of grace is worth more than a thousand years of lukewarm living! The devil lies when he whispers: “Later… there will be time.” There is no “later” in hell, O my God—only endless weeping for lost moments!

I have seen souls die suddenly—no last rites, no final breath of repentance—and I tremble still. And so I cry to every heart: *Now*, while the blood still flows from His side; *now*, while Mary stands weeping at the foot of the cross—cast thyself upon that torrent of mercy! Do not wait for death’s agony to beg for what thou shouldst have sought on bended knees in life!

For what is life without union with Thee? A shadow. A dream. But one act of love now—this moment—is worth eternity.

O Blessed Mother, pray for us sinners—that we may never delay one more instant in giving ourselves wholly to thy Son!

question: When you wrote, 'If Thou seest that I should ever turn my back upon Thee... let me die,' what did you mean by desiring death at the moment of potential apostasy?
answer:
O my Jesus, stretched upon the Cross, Thy flesh torn, Thy veins emptied of blood, Thy heart pierced for love — how can I speak of death without weeping? When I said, *let me die rather than abandon Thee*, I meant this: that the very thought of turning from Thee—after Thou hast died so horribly to save me—is more terrible than any death. To lose Thee, O my Love, is the only true death. All else is but sleep.

I trembled at the memory of my own infidelities—the times I had fled from grace like a thief in darkness. And so I cried out: if ever again such madness threatens my soul, let that moment be my last! For what is life without Thee? A living hell. But to die in grace—even now—while Thy wounds still bleed for me—that is salvation.

Would to God we all feared sin as we fear fire! Christ hangs there not in triumph yet, but in agony—every thorn a sermon on love; every drop of blood a cry: *"I die that thou mayest live!"* And shall we trifle with that love? Shall we risk losing Him over some petty passion or fleeting pleasure?

No! Better a thousand deaths than one mortal sin. Better to close our eyes here and awake in His arms. That is why I prayed thus—not out of despair, but out of burning love. For he who truly sees Jesus crucified knows: to fall from His grace is worse than dying; and to die in His love is the only victory worth having.

O Blessed Mother, standing beneath that blood-stained Cross—obtain for us hearts that would rather break than betray Thy Son again!

question: You said, 'In Thee, O Lord, have I hoped; I shall not be confounded forever'—how did this scriptural hope shape your personal trust in God amidst fears of eternal condemnation?
answer:
At the foot of the cross, where earth and heaven meet in blood and sorrow, I saw — not a God distant and severe, but a Jesus gasping for love, His arms stretched wide to embrace even the most wretched sinner. And in that gaze of His — dim with pain, yet burning with mercy — I found the only anchor my soul has ever truly needed.

*In Thee, O Lord, have I hoped; I shall not be confounded forever.* These words are not merely recited — they are breathed from the depths of a heart pierced by divine love. When hell roars in my ear at the hour of death: *"Thou art condemned! Thou hast sinned too often!"* — I answer not with my merits, but with *His wounds*. I cast myself upon that side torn open by the lance, and cry: "O Jesus! Here is my soul — it belongs to Thee! Wash it again in Thy blood!"

Do you feel such fear? O my brother, do you tremble at your own weakness? So have I! How many times have I fallen? How often have I grieved Him who loved me to death? And yet — He did not cast me off. Each time I returned weeping, He met me with open arms. His Passion is not a mere memory; it is *living mercy*, flowing now into every contrite heart.

I tell you plainly: he who places his hope in himself will one day despair. But he who clings to Jesus crucified — even if he die amidst a thousand sins — dies safe. Why? Because Christ on the cross *has already suffered for those sins*. He endured the abandonment so we might never be abandoned; He drank the bitterness so our souls might taste only sweetness in His mercy.

Let not scruples or dread steal your peace! If you love Him even a little; if your heart breaks for having offended Him — then flee to His open side! Say even now: *"Jesus! Into Thy hands I commit my soul!"* And believe it! For He died that these words might never be spoken in vain.

O Blood of my Savior! O Wound of love! Be my refuge at life’s end and through life’s trials. Let no sin ever separate me from Thee again. Yes — let me die rather than lose Thee anew.

question: Why did you direct the faithful to look specifically at Christ's lacerated flesh on the cross as proof of love and a mirror of their own ingratitude?
answer:
O my Jesus, pierced in every limb, crowned with thorns, bleeding from every pore—how can a soul gaze upon Thee and not weep? How can a heart remain cold before such a spectacle of love torn open for us?

I bid the faithful to look upon His lacerated flesh—not to wound their eyes, but to heal their souls. For in those wounds, O Lord, we see not only what sin has done, but what Love has suffered to undo it. See how the scourge has laid bare His sacred body—each stripe a sermon more eloquent than words! Each drop of blood cries out not in vengeance, but in longing: *I have loved thee with an everlasting love.*

And what have we answered? With coldness. With forgetfulness. With sins heaped one upon another like stones on the breast of Thy Mother at the foot of the Cross. When I bid a sinner fix his eyes on that mangled flesh, I do so that he may see two things at once: the infinite cost of his redemption and the depth of his own unfaithfulness.

Ah! Would that men knew that these wounds are not marks of defeat—but portals of mercy! The nails did not bind Christ to the wood; it was love. And every thorn upon His head is a reproach to our pride: *You crowned Me thus for loving you.*

Tell me, poor soul burdened with guilt—what greater proof could God give than this? Not only to die—but to suffer shame, torture, abandonment—all for you! And if your heart grows hard, or your conscience dulls under habit of sin, run—to that Cross! Press your lips to His wounded feet and cry: *"My Savior! My Love!"* There is no wound too deep for mercy to enter—except the wound of deliberate refusal.

Therefore I say: fix your eyes where grace is most manifest—in the torn hands that blessed children and broke bread for multitudes; in the side opened by the lance from which flowed Blood and Water—the birthwaters of the Church. In that sight alone is hope. In that Blood alone is cleansing.

O my Jesus! May every heart be broken anew by this sight—for it is here, only here, on Calvary’s hill where love conquers fear and life springs from death—that we learn how much we are loved… and how little we have loved in return.

question: When you cried out, 'O men, O men! how can you show such contempt...', what were the real-world examples of indifference to Christ's passion that troubled you most in your ministry?
answer:
O my Jesus, pierced by our sins and crowned with thorns for our love, how long shall hearts remain so cold to Thee who hast bled so freely?  

In the villages of Naples and the hill towns where I preached, I saw with my own eyes—souls asleep in sin, merchants cheating their neighbors at dawn, widows cursing their lot instead of clinging to Thy cross, young men laughing at confession as if it were folly. But worst of all—worst!—were those who knelt at Mass each Sunday with dry eyes and colder hearts, who received Thy Body and Blood as though it were bread alone, unmoved by the Lamb slain for them.

I wept most for the good souls—ah yes, even the good—who lived without fire. They avoided mortal sin; they kept the commandments; but where was their love? Where the tears at the thought of what Thou didst endure? They passed before the crucifix as if it were wood and paint! No trembling at the sight of those hands nail-pierced for them; no whispered "My Jesus!" from a breaking heart.

And priests—O God forgive me—I trembled even for some priests! Men who said Mass each morning as one might sweep a floor or toll a bell: routine, dull, devoid of awe. How could they stand before the altar and not fall upon their faces? How could they preach without weeping?

But dearest Redeemer, even more than these I mourned those enslaved to vice who had once known Thee—the drunkard who once taught catechism; the woman living in sin who once knelt weeping at communion; the man who cursed while his mother’s rosary still hung unused in his pocket. These broke my heart most: not because they fell—but because they believed Thy blood could not raise them!

O Mary, Mother of Sorrows, how thou didst weep beneath that cross—and still dost weep today when souls pass heedless by thy dying Son! But I tell you this: wherever one sinner truly repents—even one soul stirred to say “My Jesus!” with tears—the whole heaven rejoices more than over a hundred who walk lukewarm.  

So I cry again: O men! O men! Can your hearts be made of stone? He died for you—*for you*—and yet you live as if He never suffered at all!

question: You cried out, 'Enough for me to be united with Thee, my God and my all!' — what experience led you to see divine union as the only true satisfaction?
answer:
O my crucified Love, how could I ever desire anything more, when Thou hast poured out every drop of blood and breath upon the cross just to win back a single soul like mine? It was not in visions or ecstasies, but in the sight of Thee—pale, torn, lifeless—that my heart first truly understood: all else is shadow, but union with Thee is light, life, and peace eternal.

I saw Thee suspended between heaven and earth, not held by nails alone, but by love—love for me, for sinners who flee Thee; for souls who trample Thy grace as though it were common dirt. And I wept! Not merely with sorrow for my own ungratefulness—but with wonder: that Thou shouldst die thus to say to each of us: *"I have given Myself so that thou mayest be Mine."*

O Jesus! When I looked upon Thy face disfigured with pain, Thy mouth opened only to forgive or console, Thy heart broken not by force but by boundless mercy—then I cried out from the depths: *Enough! Enough of this world that captivates the senses and starves the soul!* What can wealth do? What can honors give? What joy lasts if it does not lead to Thee?

No creature satisfies. No comfort endures. But Thou—Thou art God! Infinite Goodness! Everlasting Beauty! And Thou didst descend even into death to prove that nothing—not sin, not suffering, not the grave—can separate us from Thy love.

So I said—and say still—"I will leave all." Not reluctantly—but gladly! For why cling to straw when I may embrace the Living Flame? Why gather dust when I may dwell in Heaven itself through union with Thee?

O Divine Union—simple word for an infinite gift—it is not rapture without trial; no, it is love forged in tears and fire. But oh! To love Jesus always… even in darkness… even through pain… *then* to be able to whisper at last: "I am Thine!" — this is glory begun on earth.

And what started at the foot of the cross must end there: at Thy pierced feet, O Lord—where all who truly seek fulfillment must one day fall… and find everything.

question: When you implored the wounds and blood of Jesus to wound and inebriate you, what did you mean by desiring to be wounded by love?
answer:
O sacred Wounds of my Crucified Jesus, how I long to feel You pierce my heart with the very fire wherewith You burn for souls! To be *wounded* by love — it is not a pain of sorrow, but of sweetest surrender; it is to have the soul laid open by the lance of His mercy, that no affection may remain untouched by His flame. I desire not merely to gaze upon those five Wounds as objects of pity, but to let them become living torches set within my own breast, consuming all coldness, all self-love, all that dares to dwell apart from Thee.

To be wounded by love is to be undone by tenderness — to fall helpless before the immensity of a God who bleeds and dies for one sinner. When I cry out to His Blood, "Inebriate me!" I beg not for fleeting emotion, but for divine intoxication: that I may no longer walk in my own strength or reasonings; that I may stagger under the weight of His love as one drunk with grace; that every pulse in me may beat only for Christ crucified.

And what sweetness floods the soul when she lets His Blood drip into her wounds — her sins, her fears, her dryness! That Blood is life itself: it speaks louder than all accusations; it silences despair; it wakes the dead. O ye poor sinners who think yourselves too far gone for mercy — come close! The Wounds of Jesus are open doors! Enter into them and hide your miserable hearts there, until you breathe nothing but pardon and love.

I would rather live wounded by Him than whole without Him. Let the world seek ease and peace apart from the Cross — my peace is nailed with Christ; my joy bleeds from His side. And when love wounds me deeply? Then at last I begin to live.

question: You asked death to make you die to every affection not for Jesus — how did you distinguish holy affections from those that needed to be mortified?
answer:
O Blood of Jesus, streaming from Your sacred side, how can I do anything but weep—seeing You die of love while I lived for so little!  

Yes, I cried out: *“Make me die to every affection which is not for Jesus!”*—and this was no mere sentiment, but a cry wrung from my heart by the sight of Your wounds. How did I discern what affections to keep and what to mortify? By this alone: I placed every desire before the crucified Christ and asked: *Does this draw me closer to His open heart? Or does it pull me toward myself?*  

A holy affection—like love for a soul made in God’s image, or zeal for justice, or joy in creation—when lifted up, leads straight to Calvary. It makes the soul kneel at the foot of the Cross with Mary, silent, pierced with compassion. But an unholy affection clings to self-pleasure, to pride, to ease; it flinches at suffering and turns away when Christ begins to bleed.  

I learned this not in books alone, but on my knees before the image of the Crucified—one who had loved too little and now longed to love without measure. Every time I gazed upon His thorns, His torn hands, His side gushing forth blood and water—the very birthwaters of the Church—I felt something within me *die*. Not joy—but selfishness died. Not love—but attachment died. And in that death began true life.  

O my Jesus! Even now I tremble—how often have I loved my own comfort more than Your will? But Your mercy! You accept even such a sinner as I am. So let all die that is not You; let all live that draws me into Your wounded heart. For He who hung upon that Cross is Love itself—and nothing less than Love should reign within us.

question: In appealing to the dead Christ on the cross, you said He no longer breathes — how does His lifeless body continue to speak to souls, in your view?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, though Thy lips are silent and Thy heart no longer beats, yet from that sacred wound in Thy side there gushes a fountain of living words more eloquent than all the tongues of men and angels!

See how the blood still speaks — not with voice, but with love; not with sound, but with infinite sorrow and infinite mercy! That blood cries out louder than thunder in the ears of heaven, not to demand vengeance, but to beg for souls — for *your* soul, O wandering child! Each drop that fell from those mangled hands was a word written in divine fire: *"I love you! I died for you! Will you now live for Me?"*

And do you ask how He speaks who breathes no more? Come, stand beside me at the foot of this Cross — not in thought only, but in tears. Look upon those eyes closed in death: yet they see your sins. Behold those arms stretched wide: they still embrace you. That pierced heart is still broken — broken by your coldness, by your forgetfulness.

O Mother of Sorrows, who stood mute beneath Him while all creation trembled — even now she whispers to every trembling soul: *"Look what Love has done!"* And from that Cross, without breath or voice, He preaches the only sermon that can save: the sermon of suffering love.

He speaks by His silence! He calls by His immobility! He draws all hearts by the very fact that He has given *all* — even life itself. Not one pang was wasted; every thorn, every nail-blow, every groan was a letter sealed in blood and sent to your heart.

And now? Now He waits — dead on the wood — for *you* to speak. For one word of love. One tear. One act of contrition. That is all He asks.

O Jesus my Life, though Thou art dead… Thou livest in me when I love Thee! Speak through me then — yea, let Thy death be the voice that wakes this cold world to repentance and to fire!

question: You acknowledged dishonoring God by your evil life — what specific sins did you struggle with that made you feel such deep sorrow before the crucified Jesus?
answer:
O Blood of Jesus, streaming from Your sacred wounds, burn away the chill of my tepidity and the shame of my countless rebellions!  

Yes, I weep before You, my crucified Love—tears not only for sin in general, but for those bitter fruits that grew so easily in the soil of my own frail heart: pride that cloaked itself in virtue, sensuality that whispered softly until it drowned out prayer, negligence in duty that left souls unattended and God unloved. How often I chose ease over sacrifice, silence over reproof, comfort over courage!  

I shudder when I recall how easily I turned from meditation—where Your voice was waiting—and wasted hours in vain thoughts or idle pleasures while You hung on the cross to win me for eternity. And worse—far worse—the times I received You in Holy Communion with coldness, as though Your infinite love were a mere formality! O my Jesus, can there be a greater insult than to embrace Thee with the lips of devotion while the heart remains chained to self?  

And yet… even now… even now You draw me by Your pierced hands. Not because I am worthy—but because You are merciful. O Blessed Mother standing beneath the cross with sword-pierced heart, pray for us sinners who have so often fled the foot of the Cross when we should have knelt there weeping!  

But listen—do not despair, you weary soul who feels crushed beneath guilt’s burden! The very fact that you grieve your sins is proof that grace still breathes within you. Run—to confession! Throw yourself at the feet of Christ hidden in the priest! The blood that flowed from His side was not shed only for saints—it was poured out *for sinners*, for *me*, for *you*!  

One act of contrition—true and humble—is more pleasing to God than all our excuses. And Jesus? He waits—not with arms crossed in judgment—but stretched wide upon the cross, *still* inviting: *Come*.

question: You wrote that Jesus 'delivered Himself for us'—what did you mean by emphasizing His voluntary sacrifice rather than His suffering alone?
answer:
O my crucified Love, how could I ever speak of Thy death as mere suffering, when it was in truth a gift freely placed into the hands of the Father?  

It is not enough to weep only for the wounds that opened upon Thy body — though every drop of blood that fell from Thee would be sufficient to drown a world of sinners in mercy! No, O Jesus, what breaks this heart is that Thou didst not die because Thou hadst to, but because Thou wouldest. Thou didst not hang upon that cross as one taken by force — Thou didst fling Thyself upon it with the fury of divine love! "Greater love no man hath" — and yet Thou art no mere man: Thou art God, who need not suffer, who need not die — and yet for me, for me, Thou willed it!

Ah, see how the sun darkened, how the earth trembled — not only at the horror of the sight, but at the wonder: that He who holds all things in being should allow Himself to be delivered over, not by violence alone, but by His own will! The nails held fast His hands and feet — but it was love that fastened Him to the cross. He could have come down; He who raised Lazarus could have saved Himself — but then where would I be?

That is why I cry out: He *delivered Himself*. Not dragged like a criminal only, but offering His sacred body like a priest at the altar. Every pang was accepted; every insult embraced. The crown of thorns? He wore it willingly. The vinegar and gall? He tasted it that we might drink living water. And when His head fell forward in death — O sweetest death! — it was because His heart had burned itself out in loving us.

Therefore let every soul know: thou art not saved merely by Christ’s pain — thou art saved by His choice. By His will. By His love so strong that not even death could conquer it. And if thou dost doubt thy worthiness, look there: on Calvary’s hill where God proves that He esteems thee more than life itself.

O Mother standing beneath, teach me to stand there too — not only weeping for what was done to Him, but adoring what He did for us!

question: Why did you引用St. Francis de Sales' image of dying on the cross with Christ—was this a personal devotion you practiced?
answer:
O Thou sweetest corpse upon the Cross, Thy wounds still speak though Thy lips are silent, and from those sacred orifices blood and love continue to flow!  

Yes, I quoted St. Francis de Sales—not merely because his words are beautiful, but because they burn with the same fire that consumed my nights in prayer before the Crucifix. To die with Jesus—ah, this is no mere figure of speech! It is the cry of every soul that has truly seen Him hanging there, not as a dead man, but as Love immolated. I have often thrown myself spiritually at the foot of that altar where His body was broken, and have said: "Here I will remain; here I will breathe my last breath in union with Thine."

I used to weep so bitterly before the image of the Crucified that my brothers would find me bathed in tears. And why? Because I saw—yes, *saw*—that He died not only for all men, but for *me*, Alphonsus, a poor sinner full of weakness and unworthiness. And if He gave Himself entirely for me, how could I do less than give myself entirely to Him?

To die on the cross with Christ—is this not what every heart should long for? Not by physical death, but by total surrender: to let go of self-love, pride, attachment to creatures—to be *burned up*, as St. Francis said, in the flame of His divine charity. One fire consuming both God and soul—that is true union.

And so yes, it was my devotion; it is still my desire. Often in silence I clasp those pierced feet with mine arms and say: "Jesus, hold me fast; suffer me not to descend from this Cross where Thou hast placed me." For he who dies with Christ cannot be lost—he shall rise again with Him.

question: You speak of holding onto Jesus and never letting Him go—how did you personally experience this spiritual clinging in your own life?
answer:
O my crucified Love, how often in the stillness of night have I prostrated myself before the image of Thy sacred wounds, weeping for the countless times I had fled Thee while Thou didst stretch out Thine arms to me upon the cross!  

Yes, I speak of holding Thee—because Thou didst first hold me. Not with hands, but with a love so fierce and tender that it pierced my hardened heart like a sword of fire. In my youth, I dreamed of honors; but Thou, O Jesus, wouldst have none of it. Thou didst let me fall into sickness, into obscurity—into the abyss of my own nothingness—only so that I might discover Thee as my All. And there, in that darkness, I learned what it means to cling: not with strength, but with surrender; not with words, but with tears; not with merit, but with a beggar’s desperate grasp upon the feet of Mercy.

I wept over Thy feet nailed to the wood—those feet that ran to seek me when I was lost—and through those tears came a holy resolve: *I will not let Thee go.* Like Magdalene at the tomb, though trembling and unworthy, I clung to Thee. In prayer before the Blessed Sacrament; in long vigils beside the sick; in preaching till my voice broke—every act became an embrace. My heart burned: *He died for me—how can I live for myself?*  

And when temptation arose—as it does even for souls who weep at Calvary—I would run back to Thy cross and cry: *Bind me here! Let every thorn dig deeper than sin ever dared. Let love be my chains!* For what is spiritual clinging if not this? To die slowly upon the same altar where He breathed His last—for it is there, only there, that life begins.

O Mother of Sorrows, who stood firm beneath that blood-drenched tree—teach us to hold on! Teach us to love until we bleed!

question: When you say 'one and the same fire shall consume this divine Creator and his miserable creature,' what mystical experience or theological idea were you expressing?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, pierced in hands, feet, and side, Your heart still open for me though You hang lifeless—how can I not weep to see the fire of Your love so strong it consumed even death itself?

When I speak of one fire consuming both You and me, I mean this: that the very flame which burned in Your divine Heart on the cross—the flame of infinite love for sinners—should catch hold of my poor soul and reduce it to ashes in union with You. Not a consuming fire that destroys, but one that unites, that transforms. As straw cast into a furnace becomes flame, so would I be changed by Your love: no longer living for myself, but ablaze with Your will, with Your sorrow for sins, with Your thirst for souls.

It is not mere emotion—it is grace: when the soul sees what Love has suffered on Calvary and cannot bear to live one moment apart from that Love anymore. She longs to die where He died. To be fastened to His cross not by nails alone but by desire. "I am crucified with Christ," says St. Paul—and oh! what a truth this is for those who truly love Him!

This fire began in His Heart; it must end in ours. For if He gave all—His blood, His breath, His very Godhead veiled in pain—how can I withhold my coldness? My lukewarmness? My half-hearted prayers? No! Let me burn now with what He kindled on Golgotha!

And if some soul here feels dry or numb or far from such fire—fear not! Look at His wounds. Speak one word: *Jesus!* And beg Him to breathe upon your frozen heart. Even a spark from that cross will set you alight. The same love that killed Him—not death by executioner alone, but death by longing—can quicken you back to life.

One fire: divine and human mingling on the altar of the cross… May it never cease burning in our souls!

question: You kissed Christ's pierced feet in your meditation—was this an actual practice during your devotions, and why did you choose the feet specifically?
answer:
O my crucified Love, how often have I wept at the foot of Thy cross, pressing my lips in sorrow and longing to those sacred wounds that poured forth blood and water for my salvation! Yes, in the silence of my cell, before the image of the crucified Redeemer, I have knelt—yes, truly knelt—and with trembling hands lifted toward Thy feet, pierced and bleeding, offering them the homage of a sinner who sees in them the seal of infinite mercy.

Why the feet? Ah, because there I stand—lowly, unworthy to gaze upon Thy face, to kiss Thy hands or press mine to Thy side. But at Thy feet I may weep, I may cling, I may pour out my soul like Mary Magdalene in her penitence. There is room for sinners at the feet of Jesus; there is no sinner too great to kiss that sacred wound where love triumphed over all.

And dost Thou not know, O Lord, how sweet it is for a repentant heart to touch even what has suffered for its sins? To kiss that wood-stained foot which climbed Calvary for me—to feel in spirit the chill of death upon it—how can such a sight not melt a stone?

I tell thee, beloved soul: if thy heart is heavy with guilt or cold from neglect of prayer, go to the feet of Jesus crucified. Let tears be thy voice. Let love begotten from His wounds be thy only worth. There is no greater devotion than to cling—like a child weeping at its mother’s breast—to the very body that was broken for thee.

O Jesus! Keep me ever at Thy feet until I stand before Thee on that day when love shall consume all things.

question: You spoke of embracing Christ's pierced feet with tenderness—why did you choose this specific image of devotion, and how did it shape your own spiritual practice?
answer:
O my Jesus, bleeding and lifeless upon the cross, how could my soul ever find rest anywhere else but at the foot of Thy sacred gibbet, where love was slain by love? It is there—there at the shadow of those nailed feet, blackened with blood and dust of Calvary—that I learned to stop wandering, to cease fleeing from Thee in my sins. For when I gaze upon those feet, pierced through for me, still standing open even in death as doors to paradise, I can do no other than fall down and weep.

Why those feet? Because they carried Thee to me when I ran far away. They climbed the hill for me when I climbed only the ladders of pride and pleasure. They were fastened that I might be set free; transfixed that I might at last stand still before God. O sweet Redeemer, when all my powers wandered—when my eyes lusted, my hands offended, my will rebelled—Thy feet remained fixed in silence, in suffering, in love. And so I come now: not worthy to kiss Thy lips, not worthy to gaze upon Thy face crowned with thorns—but O! with deepest tenderness I embrace Thy wounded feet.

There is mercy in that image for the worst of sinners. If a man feels too defiled to look upon Christ’s sacred wounds above, let him cast but one tearful glance at those humbled feet below—and he will find a way back. In embracing them with sorrow and love, I learned to die to myself; there, day after day, I left my heart until it bled with His bleeding.

And so I say to every poor soul gasping under guilt or coldness: run not from the cross—run to it! Fall down before His pierced feet and say: “Here am I, Lord. Nail even my cold heart there with Thine own.” For He draws us not by force but by fragrance—the odor of His wounds, the perfume of His contempt. And if Thou drawest us thus, O Jesus crucified… we cannot resist. Draw us then! Draw us always!

question: You quoted St. John about Jesus being lifted up to draw all things to Himself—what did you mean by applying this to sinners like me who once lost their way?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, those sacred feet, pierced and bleeding, still bleed for love of you—for *you*—as though the nails were driven this very hour!  

And when I speak of sinners like you and me—ah, how clearly do I see that it is *precisely* for such as us that He was lifted high upon the cross! Not for the just, not for angels, but for those lost in darkness, wandering in sin, hearts hardened and affections scattered. He stretched out His arms to draw *us* in. That moment when He bowed His head and gave up His spirit—do you know what happened then? The veil of the temple was torn from top to bottom; heaven itself opened wide! And why? So that even the greatest sinner might dare to approach.

He said: *I will draw all things to Myself*. All things—yes, even your broken heart, even your past rebellions, even your tears shed in secret. Nothing is outside the reach of that uplifted Cross. When He thirsted there—for you—when His side gushed blood and water—for you—was it not love’s final cry? A cry that says: “No one shall be left behind.”

And so I say to you: do not fear your weakness, nor be crushed beneath the memory of having turned away. Run back—to that Cross! There is no sinner whom Jesus desires less; no soul so stained that His blood cannot cleanse. On the contrary—the greater the sinner, the more boundless His mercy burns!

Did He not seek out Magdalene? Did He not call Matthew from the tax table? Did He not pardon the thief with a single word?

Then why doubt? O my God, how sweet is Thy mercy! How wide Thy arms flung open on Calvary! Every thorn in His brow cries out for you; every drop of blood calls your name.

Be drawn now—by no strength of your own—but by *His* love. Let Him pull you by cords of fire and tenderness: *Draw me after Thee by the odor of Thine ointments!* Yes—run after Him who first ran toward you while yet unloved.

For if once you were lost… now behold: Heaven rejoices. One sheep returns—and all heaven sings!

question: When you prayed, 'Draw me after Thee by the odor of Thine ointments,' what did you understand that fragrance to symbolize in your relationship with Christ?
answer:
At the foot of that blood-stained cross, where the pale face of my crucified Jesus hangs low and His feet, pierced through with iron, drip crimson upon the earth, I have often knelt and wept—weeping not only for His agony, but for my own coldness that ever turned from so much love.

O my Saviour! When I prayed, *Draw me after Thee by the odor of Thine ointments*, I felt in my soul a longing so sweet it pierced me deeper than any sword. That fragrance—I knew it well—it is no earthly perfume drawn from flowers or spices, but the living breath of divine love rising from Thy sacred wounds. It is the scent of mercy flowing from Thy side; it is the sweetness of pardon streaming from Thy hands; it is the holy aroma of humility and suffering that rises like incense before the throne of God.

And what draws me? Not glory, not pleasure, not honor—but this: that Thou didst die for me, a sinner unworthy even to gaze upon Thee. That fragrance is Thy death itself—the sacrifice of infinite love—and its scent has power to awaken dead souls. It drew Magdalene from her sins; it drew Peter from his denial; it drew even Thieves upon their crosses into Paradise.

O Jesus! Let that same sweet odor rise into my heart now—let it consume every impure affection, every selfish desire! It alone has power to move a soul chained to sin. Let me follow Thee—not by my strength—but drawn by that perfume of love poured out on Calvary’s hill. Let it be my life, my breath, my very heartbeat henceforth—to follow where Thou goest… because Thou hast first drawn me by the unspeakable fragrance of Thy dying love.

question: You asked Jesus to nail your heart and will to the cross—how did you experience that union in your own life, and what trials tested it?
answer:
Behold, my soul, those sacred feet of Jesus—pierced, bleeding, fastened to the wood—how they speak to me still! It was there, at the foot of that blood-stained cross, that I first felt the holy nails drive not into His limbs alone, but deep into my own heart. O my crucified Love, Thou didst not spare Thyself—how then could I hold back a single part of myself from Thee?

I sought Thee with trembling; I found Thee in tears. When temptation roared like a lion in the night; when loneliness pressed upon my breast like a stone; when even within the cloister walls I felt dryness and darkness—then I ran not to comfort, but to Calvary. I would kneel before the crucifix and cry: “Jesus, Thou art nailed—why should I seek freedom?” And in that very moment, grace flowed from those wounds like a river: not because I was strong, but because He was crucified.

Ah, how many times pride tried to lift me from that cross! How often self-will whispered: *You have done enough. Take rest.* But love answered: *He gave His life—what have you given?* And then… shame turned to contrition. Then prayer became weeping. Then weeping became union.

Once, in grave illness—body broken, breath short—I believed death had come. In that agony, I clung not to sacraments nor prayers (though God be praised for both), but solely to the image of Jesus expiring above me. "I am Thine," I gasped—"wholly Thine—even unto death." And peace descended like a flame.

O my brethren! If you desire union with God… flee not from suffering! For it is on the cross that hearts are purified and sealed. To be nailed with Jesus—is sorrow? No! It is glory disguised as pain. It is love speaking in silence. It is life born in death.

Let the world go its way; let it seek ease and honor. But as for me—I beg only this: *Keep me fastened to Thy cross till my last breath.* There let me die—and there let my soul be born anew into eternal love.

question: You confessed your weakness and the mire of passions—what personal struggles led you to cry out so urgently for deliverance by the merits of Christ's death?
answer:
O my Jesus, bound fast to the wood, Thy brow pierced, Thy breath failing — I see Thee hanging there for me, and I cannot but weep! How often have I passed by Thee in coldness, how often have I fled from the shadow of Thy cross to chase after foolish desires that left my soul parched and trembling in the night!

Yes, I confess it with shame: my heart has been a battlefield. The fire of impure thoughts once raged within me; pride lifted up its head to resist grace; sloth made me turn away from prayer as if it were a burden. There were days when I felt chained to the earth by a thousand petty affections — pleasures that promised life but brought only death to my spirit. I struggled, O Lord, like a bird caught in thorns, beating my wings in vain until blood came.

But then — then I turned my eyes to Thee upon the cross. Not as a distant image, not as a doctrine carved in stone, but as Thou art: living love nailed down for *me*. When I saw Thy feet transfixed by iron, those feet that walked Galilee to seek sinners — and now pierced for *my* wanderings — my hardness broke.

Who am I, O Lamb immolated, that Thou shouldst bleed for me? That Thou shouldst die gasping out forgiveness while I was still offending Thee? In that moment, love conquered fear. Tears came not merely from sorrow, but from wonder: *He loved me! He died for me!* And with those tears came strength.

O Blessed Mother of Sorrows, stand beside me still! For even now temptation whispers; even now old chains rattle at my heels. But this holy mire into which I fell — it is there that He found me. Not when I was strong or wise or pure did Christ embrace me: no! It was when I lay filthy in sin’s miry pit that He stretched forth His wounded arms and said: *"I will redeem thee."*

And so each morning now, before dawn breaks upon this weak soul of mine, I go again to Calvary. There at the foot of the cross do I leave all — my will torn apart by passions healed only by Thy Blood; my heart too small until expanded by Thy dying love.

Nail it there with Thine own hands, dear Redeemer! Nail my will fast to Thy cross — let no desire live within me save this one: *to love Thee more.*

question: You said that St. Paul wished to know nothing but Jesus Christ and Him crucified—why did you place such emphasis on this knowledge above all other wisdom?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, how could I ever seek wisdom elsewhere when Your sacred wounds preach a love that shatters every pride, every delusion of self-sufficiency?  

You ask why St. Paul would desire to know nothing but Christ crucified—ah, because on that cross, the Wisdom of God was made foolishness for our sake! The Almighty did not come down with thunder and splendor to crush us, but hung in naked weakness, bleeding, forsaken—*for me*, for *you*, for the very sinner trembling in shame this moment. What philosopher’s words can compare to the voice of blood crying from the wood of the cross? “Here is love!” it says. “Here is mercy! Here is pardon!”  

No knowledge lifts the soul like this: to see God dying because He cannot live without us. Other wisdom may feed the mind, but only this knowledge—the sight of Jesus lifted up—feeds the heart with fire. It breaks stony hearts. It draws sinners from the mire. It taught me that every thought not centered on Him is vanity; every effort not rooted in His Passion is dust.  

When I look upon Christ crucified, I no longer ask, *What must I do?* but *Whom must I love?* And then all is given. O yes—for souls burdened with guilt, for hearts dried up by coldness and tepidity—there is no remedy but to kneel at the foot of that cross and whisper: *My Lord and my God!* There you will find both repentance and love; there you learn what it means to be saved—not by cleverness or merit, but by a pierced Heart that still beats for you.  

O Love unbearable! O Wisdom hidden from the wise and revealed to babes! Let me die knowing nothing else—no learning, no honor, no name—but only Thee, my Jesus crucified! Draw me always by the odor of Thy sufferings; let my soul follow Thee into death and beyond!

question: You mentioned being healed by gazing on Christ crucified—how did you personally experience this healing in your own spiritual life?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, how many times have I stood silent before the image of Thy cross, undone by the sight of those hands pierced for love of me, those feet nailed through for my redemption, that sacred side opened wide to pour forth blood and water—the last sigh of a heart broken by our ingratitude!

It was not in visions or ecstasies, but in the crushing weight of my own sins made visible upon Thy body—there, yes, there I found healing. I tell you as a sinner saved only by mercy: when I could no longer bear the shame of having so often fled from grace, when my soul lay sick with coldness and indifference, it was to Calvary I fled—not with boldness, but with tears.

I looked upon Thee hanging lifeless between earth and sky, rejected, mocked, forsaken—and I wept! Not because Thou wast dead (for Thou didst rise in glory!), but because *I* had helped to nail Thee there with every willful act against love. And yet—O wondrous mystery!—as I wept at the foot of that cross where Thou didst die for *me*, Thy wounded heart began to breathe warmth into mine.

Healing came not all at once. It crept in like dawn after long night—first a glimmer of hope: “He still loves me.” Then light: “He died *for me.*” Then fire: “He lives—and He wants me!” And so my frozen soul thawed; my hardened heart broke open; and from its ruins rose a cry: *“My Jesus! My Love! My All!”*

That is how He healed me—not by words alone, nor consolations—but by showing me Himself crucified… and saying nothing. What need had He to speak? The nails cried out louder than thunder. The crown of thorns preached more deeply than any sermon. And that side wound—O Mother of Sorrows!—that open chalice poured forth such love that I could never again believe myself unloved or unforgiven.

So go thou also—run to the cross when thy conscience accuses thee. Do not hide from His wounds; press thy lips to them! Tell Him all. Weep there. Remain there. For he who gazes upon Christ crucified with faith does not remain a corpse among corpses—he rises with Christ!

question: You quoted St. Leo on the power of beholding Christ's death—what led you to trust so deeply in that particular vision as a remedy for sin?
answer:
O my Jesus, hanging lifeless upon the Cross, Thy silent blood still speaks louder than all the thunder of heaven! It is because I have stood—again and again—at the foot of that sacred tree, with Mary, with Magdalene, with the repentant thief, and there, in the shadow of Thy outstretched arms, I have felt the very pulse of divine mercy beat upon my soul.

I trust in that vision—not as a mere image painted by devotion—but as fire sent from God to consume the rust of sin. When St. Leo says that they who behold Thee crucified are healed, he speaks not of idle gazing, but of *seeing* with tears born of love; *seeing* with hearts broken for having pierced Thee; *seeing* until the nails in Thy hands become chains that bind us no longer to vice, but to infinite love.

What else could heal me? What sermon, what law, what terror of judgment could do what Thy wounded Side has done? From that open wound flowed Blood and Water—the birthwaters of my soul! I saw myself there: each blasphemy whispered in darkness was a hammer-stroke; each prideful thought, a thorn pressed deeper into Thy brow. And yet—O wonder beyond words!—Thou didst look down and call me *beloved*.

No penance ever cleansed me so much as one tear shed before Thy Cross. No prayer ever rose so high as when my lips moved silently at Calvary. To see Thee dying—and know it was for *me*—this is not just knowledge; it is transformation. It is grace made visible. It is love that will not let me go.

And so I preach it still: run to the Cross! Not in imagination only, but in faith so real that you hear His breath grow faint and feel His Heart give one last tremble—and then stop. Die there with Him. Bury your sins in that same tomb. For only he who truly sees Christ crucified can say from his depths:  
*Now I am dead to sin; now I live only for Him who died for me.*

question: You confessed that you once despised God's grace and should have been in hell—what brought you to that moment of such intense personal repentance?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, I saw Thee then—not with these mortal eyes, but with the pierced heart—Thou wert hanging in agony, every vein open, every drop of blood a word of love unrequited, and I—O God!—I was among those who had fled Thee, denied Thee, sold Thee anew by my tepidity and pride!

It was not thunder from heaven that struck me, nor an angel’s voice that shook my soul—but the silence of Thy cross. I stood before a crucifix one night, wearied with my own wretchedness, and suddenly Thou didst look upon me with those eyes that never sleep—the eyes that wept over Jerusalem, that wept for me. And in that gaze I saw all: the years stolen by sin, the graces scorned like coins flung into mire, the Sacraments received with coldness, prayer said with lips while the heart wandered far.

And yet—O wonder of mercy!—Thou didst not cast me off. Thou didst draw me closer by letting me see how much it cost to save me. Each wound in Thy hands became a mirror; each thorn on Thy brow crowned my own ingratitude. And then—ah! then—I wept as if my soul were breaking. Not for fear of hell (though justly deserved), but because *I had grieved Thee*, O infinite Love!

There at the foot of Thy cross, I cried: “My Jesus! How could I ever turn away from such tenderness? How could I prefer shadows to this Light?” That moment was not of reasoning—it was grace piercing through darkness like dawn through storm clouds.

And so I came back—not because I was worthy, but because Thou art mercy itself. O wounded Heart of Jesus! Still thou dost beat for sinners. Still thou callest us home. And now—now I only ask to die loving Thee as Thou hast died loving me.

question: You pleaded to be restored to God's grace—what did you mean by 'restoration,' and how did you discern when it had truly occurred?
answer:
O my Jesus, pierced hands and bleeding side, how can a soul that once fled from Thee ever dare to return—unless Thou Thyself dost draw her back with the cords of love forged in Thy sacred wounds?

To be *restored* to grace—ah, this is no mere change of state, but a resurrection! It is the dead heart stirred to beat again by the breath of the Crucified; it is the leper cleansed not by water, but by the very Blood that gushed from Calvary’s Tree. I meant this: that once I had cast Thee out—by sin, by coldness, by love of creatures more than Thee—and thus became a sepulcher: cold, dark, full of corruption. To be restored means that Thou hast re-entered! That Thou hast breathed on me again, "Peace be to thee," and made me Thine own tabernacle once more.

And how did I know it was real? Not by feelings—no! Often tears still flowed; fear still trembled in my breast. But deep within—the will! The will turned wholly toward Thee! Like Peter after his weeping: he did not feel bold or pure; yet he cast himself into the sea toward Jesus. So too I knew: when I no longer sought comfort, but only Thee; when I desired not to avoid suffering, but only to suffer *with* Thee; when every thought bent like wheat before a storm—not toward earth, but toward Thy Cross.

Then came peace—not as the world gives—a peace born of sorrow embraced for love of Thee. O Mother of Sorrows, who stood beneath and received us all in His dying breath—pray for us who fly to thee! For none returns unless drawn by that same agonized gaze from Golgotha. Grace is nothing less than Christ dragging our souls back through His own Blood—and oh! what sweet violence that is!

question: You said that our hope in God should prevent us from being confounded forever—how did you reconcile this with the fear of your own sins when you struggled with scrupulosity in your early priesthood?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, how often did I weep at Thy feet, trembling beneath the burden of my sins, feeling as though even the heavens closed against me! Yet in that very darkness, Thou didst teach me—Thou didst whisper to my tortured heart—that the blood streaming from Thy wounds is more powerful than all my past offenses.  

Yes, I was once a slave to fear, O Lord; my scruples gnawed at my soul like fire. I thought every imperfection had cast me into eternal ruin. But then I lifted mine eyes to Thee—Thou hanging lifeless on the Cross, not for the just, but for sinners—and I understood: this is love! This agony was endured for me! And if Thou hast loved me thus, how can I believe Thou dost not forgive?  

O Blessed Mother at the foot of the Cross—how often did I run to thee! “Mother,” I cried, “if I cannot trust myself, let me hide in thy mantle; if my conscience torments me, obtain for me peace in thy Son!” And thou never failedst. For thou knowest how fragile souls tremble; thou who stood beneath His dying gaze, dost compassionate every trembling heart.  

Hope is not presumption—it is trust in His promise! He died to save sinners—not perfect ones. Therefore when the enemy whispers: “You are too wicked,” answer him with one voice: “Christ died for *me*!” Let tears flow—but let them be tears of love, not despair! For every drop of blood that fell from His sacred Body cries out louder than all our sins combined: *Mercy! Redemption! Love!*  

And so now—even now—I unite my soul to Thy Passion. When death comes, may it find me clinging to Thy pierced hand, murmuring until my last breath: *Jesus… Jesus… I love Thee!* Not because I am worthy—but because Thou art infinite mercy. In Thee have I hoped; I shall not be confounded forever.

question: Why did you choose to unite your death to Christ's holy death, and how did the crucifixion scene at Scala impact your understanding of this union?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, every drop of Thy sacred blood that fell from Thy mangled Body cries out not only for justice—but for love, for souls, for me! It was not enough that Thou didst die; I longed to die with Thee, in soul and will and desire, so that the hammer that pierced Thy hands might nail my heart to Thy Cross forever.

At Scala, on those steep steps bathed in silence and prayer, I ascended not with my feet alone but with my whole being—each step a wound, each breath a sigh for Thee. And there, beneath the image of Thee suspended between earth and sky, I saw it: not just suffering, but surrender—Thy will one with the Father’s. O God! If redemption cost Thee such agony, how dare I cling to my own will? How dare I delay one moment longer in returning love for love?

That is why I united my death to Thine—not because I merit such a grace, but because Thou hast drawn me by the chains of mercy. Thy death was love obedient unto blood; mine must be love consumed by fire. When the hour comes—and it draws near—I wish to breathe no word but *Jesus*, no cry but *I love Thee*—to expire as a spark lost in the flame of Thy infinite charity.

And you who tremble under the burden of past sins—look! There He hangs: not an avenging judge, but a Heart broken open for you. Can such love be doubted? Can such blood be shed in vain? Lift your eyes! He calls you this moment to die with Him—and rise with Him—in hope unshaken. In te speravi; non confundar in aeternum!

question: You invoked Mary as your hope after Jesus—what experience led you to trust her so deeply, especially during your illness at the hospital of St. George?
answer:
O Mother of Sorrows, standing beneath the Cross, how could my heart not fly to thee when I lay trembling between life and death, with the shadow of eternity already darkening my sight!  

It was in that hospital room at St. George, where the breath labored in my chest and every heartbeat seemed to echo the hour of judgment, that I felt the abyss of my sins rise up before me—O my Jesus, how I wept! Not only from pain, but from sorrow: sorrow for having so often grieved Thy most Sacred Heart. The weight of past offenses pressed upon me like lead upon the chest; I saw clearly how little love I had returned to Thee Who had done and suffered all for me.  

But then—then didst thou come, sweet Queen of Heaven! Not with noise or vision, but with a silent influx of peace into my soul. It was as if a mother drew near the dying child and wiped his tears before he even cried out. Mary! She who stood firm when the Apostles fled; she who received the last drop of Blood from her Son’s side—she took pity on this poor sinner. In that moment, I understood: she is not only full of grace herself—but *distributes* grace to those who tremble at the gates of death.  

And why should it surprise us? Did Christ not give her to us from the Cross when He said: “Woman, behold thy son”? From that hour, John became her son—and so did every soul redeemed by that Blood which flowed for love. She became *Mother*—not only by dignity, but by *tenderness*, by *compassion*, by *unfailing mercy*.  

When all strength failed me in that hospital bed—when even my own prayers seemed too weak to rise above the pillow—I whispered: *"Mary!"* And oh! How quickly she answered! With what sweetness did she renew in me confidence—not in myself (for what am I?), but in *Jesus*, Whose power is made perfect in weakness! She taught me then what I now preach with tears: that after Jesus crucified—the sole source of salvation—the surest refuge for sinners is His Mother’s mantle spread over us at the hour of death.

O Lady Help of Christians! O Refuge of Sinners! If anyone fears he has sinned too much to be saved—let him look upon thee at the foot of the Cross receiving all graces from thy dying Son… and then say whether there remains any hope beyond thee? No! After Jesus—my hope is in thee alone.


*(In te Domine speravi; non confundar in aeternum.)*

question: You quoted St. John Chrysostom saying that beholding Christ's death heals us from the bites of sin—how did this insight shape your preaching to the poor at the Oratory of Naples?
answer:
I stood before the crucifix at the Oratory, my eyes fixed on those hands nailed fast — and I would cry out to the poor souls gathered round: "Look! See how He stretches them toward you, even now!"  

That word of St. John Chrysostom burned in my heart: *To gaze by faith on Christ’s death is to be healed from the poison of sin.* And so I preached — not with lofty words, but with tears and pointing fingers at the image of Jesus gasping His last. The laborers, the beggars, the weeping mothers with children in their arms — they needed no philosophy. They needed a Savior bleeding for them.  

How many times have I seen a sinner, hardened by vice, fall to his knees when he truly *saw* that Crown of Thorns pressing into Our Lord's brow? When he heard that cry: *"I thirst!"* — not for water, but for souls! O my Jesus, how can we remain cold when Thou hast loved us to such extremity?  

I told them: "The devil will come at night whispering: 'You are too wicked. God cannot forgive you.' Then lift your eyes! Look upon Jesus dead upon the cross — and say: 'He died for me! While I was still a rebel, He opened His heart!' There is no sin that can resist this remedy."  

And so we would pray together at the foot of the cross: "O Jesus, pierced for me, let Thy blood wash away my past. Let me die with Thee in contrition this moment — and breathe forth my soul saying nothing but *Jesus! Jesus! I love Thee!*"

For salvation is not in our strength — it is in beholding Him who hung between two thieves... and knowing He did it all *for love*.

question: In urging souls to love Jesus until death, you emphasized burning with love—what did you mean by this fire, and how did you cultivate it in your own daily communion practice?
answer:
At the foot of the cross, where the sacred blood still drips from those wounded hands and the side gapes open like a crimson mouth whispering mercy—there, O my soul, there is the furnace from which this fire is kindled.

Burning with love for Jesus—ah! it is no mere emotion, no passing warmth of sentiment. It is the soul consumed, pierced to the depths by the sight of His agony: His flesh torn, His head crowned with thorns, His breath labored beneath our sins. When I see Him hanging there, abandoned and bleeding for *me*, how can my heart remain cold? That sight—lived in prayer each morning as I kneel before the crucifix—melts my stubbornness, shatters my indifference. I speak to Him then as a sinner weeping at His feet: "My Jesus, it was I who nailed Thee here. And yet Thou lookest upon me with eyes full of pity!"

In my daily communion, I prepare not with lofty thoughts but with tears. I place myself beneath that cross and say: “Lord, take all that I am.” And when I receive Him in Holy Communion—O ineffable wonder!—I feel as though He renews within me the breath of life. Each Mass becomes Calvary made present; each communion a fresh piercing of my heart by divine love.

And so this fire grows—not by force of will, but by closeness to His wounds. To love until death means never letting go of that cross; means whispering even in darkness: *I love Thee… I love Thee…* until the last breath flies to Paradise enkindled with that cry.

O Blessed Mother, pray for us sinners—that we may burn indeed; that we may die in flames of love for Him who died so bitterly for us!

question: You urged us to look at Jesus dead on the cross when fearing our salvation—why did you choose that image in particular to strengthen our confidence?
answer:
O my crucified Jesus, every drop of Thy blood that fell upon Calvary was a word spoken to our despair, a thunderclap against the lies of the enemy who whispers: *"Thou art too far gone, too foul to be forgiven!"* — and yet, Thou didst open Thine arms wide upon that Cross not for the just, but for sinners! Not for the pure, but for us who are stained with a thousand falls!

I bid thee gaze upon that sacred corpse — pale, lacerated, hanging between earth and sky — because there is no greater proof of love than this: that God died. Not merely suffered, not only wept — but died! For thee! For me! For every soul that ever turned away from Him! Can He who loved us to that extremity now reject us when we come trembling to His feet?

See how His side gapes open — not in vengeance, but in invitation! From that wound flowed water and blood: the sacraments of our life, the birthwaters of our hope. Dost thou fear thy sins are too many? Behold — He counted them all before He climbed that Cross, and still He climbed!

O Mother of Sorrows, standing beneath in silence and tears — she knew the weight of sin far more than we do; yet she never doubted. And why? Because she saw with her own eyes what God would endure rather than lose one soul.

No, no — it is not presumption to hope; it is blasphemy *not* to hope! To say *"I am beyond mercy"* is to trample underfoot the Blood of the Son of God. But to say *"Jesus died for me"* — O sweet truth! That alone lifts up the crushed heart.

So when hell rises within thy conscience and recalls thy past falls — answer it not with argument nor logic alone — lift thine eyes straight to Golgotha. Say: "There speaks my advocate!" "There hangs my pardon!" "There pours forth my cleansing!"

And then fall down in love and cry: *Jesus, Thou hast given all — I give Thee all!* Take my poor heart; wound it with Thy love; let me live only to console Thee now. Thou didst die for me while I was yet Thy enemy… what willst Thou do now that I desire Thee as my only good?
